next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 15

Margot's legs were fine.

In the grey light before the sun cleared the eastern ridge, she sat on the edge of her bed with both feet flat on the cold tile, and her legs were fine. She felt the chill through her soles, the mattress edge against the backs of her thighs, the small adjustments in her calves that kept her upright. Everything worked. It had always worked.

Under the tile and the mattress and the ordinary fact of a body sitting on a bed at six in the morning was Tomas's phantom. Through the feed she had felt his neural bridge break. Full-resolution sensation came down the bidirectional path between her nervous system and his: compression in the lower spine, signals running through his legs, then nothing at all. The stop at the waist had been clean and dead.

On the third step the phantom hit, a skip in the signal, a gap as if her left leg's proprioception blanked for one fraction of a second. Her hand shot to the desk. The gap closed. Another step. Fine. Another. Fine. On the seventh step the right leg vanished the same way and her weight pitched sideways until the wall caught her.

She stayed there until the wave passed. Tomas was in the medical bay paralyzed from the waist down, and she was standing in her own room waiting for borrowed deadness to drain out of working legs.

The feed interface sat on the nightstand where she'd dropped it. Her hand closed around it before she'd decided to move. The sensor pad fit her palm. At once the inside of her right wrist went alert, skin remembering the pressure of the clasp. Saliva gathered under her tongue. H...
open_hooks_to_carry: Tomas's blackout during hurdles: spinal impact, neural bridge destroyed, paralysis from the waist down
Margot in the feed during the crash—she experiences the impact at full sensory resolution and disconnects screaming
Kai discovers Yusuf's encrypted archive in the calibration bay

payoffs_to_land.description: Kai discovers Yusuf's unauthorized personal data archive

prev_tail_excerpt:
For the first ten meters they held even. Then Dara's stroke length started buying distance. At the far wall Dara flipped while Lena still had three strokes left. By the time Lena turned, white feet were already leaving the other lane. Dara touched at the near wall, lifted her goggles, and waited while Lena took the last five strokes in alone.

"Five seconds," Dara said when Lena reached the wall. "Easy pace."

Water ran off Dara's chin. She did not wipe it away.

"Why did you do it?" she asked. "Not the patch. This part."

Lena kept one hand on the wall. "The desync was taking time I was alive for. I was in the pool and then I wasn't, and I couldn't tell you where the missing part went."

"But the body kept swimming."

"Yes."

"And it was —" She glanced at the lane between them, at the water Lena had taken longer to cross. "Your times were there. When it was doing that."

"It wasn't me."

"Five seconds, though." Dara looked at the pace clock, then back. "At easy."

"I know."

Dara pulled at the frayed elastic on her wrist.

"Okay," she said at last. "I hope it holds."

Then she pulled her goggles back down and left on her interval, first stroke in place before Lena had let go of the wall.

Lena stayed at the wall until the red hand reached the top of the pace clock again. Dara was already halfway down the pool. When Lena pushed off, the lane beside her was empty. Midway through the length the wall came a stroke later than she had judged. She took the extra stroke, turned, and kept going.
secondary_character_beats: Tomas's injury is rendered as the destruction of a specific young life: his career, his family's income in Quito, his body's entire purpose, gone in a fraction of a second
state_deltas_required: Everything converges: the virus strikes during training, destroying a life and exposing every concealment
A real person is permanently destroyed; Margot's addiction and Yusuf's conspiracy both surface under crisis pressure; trust dissolves
